<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These men were trusting enough to set out on a journey to a faraway land following nothing more than a star.<|8.56|> <|9.48|> They knelt before a mere journey home by a completely different path.<|12.72|> <|13.68|> They had no hope.<|15.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> of comfort in retracing their steps,<|3.06|> <|3.46|> no whole new route.<|19.58|> <|20.64|> And they did it.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We don't know if they complained or argued among themselves, I would guess they probably did, I would, about whether they really needed to do it this way.<|11.34|> <|11.92|> The writer of Matthew just tells us they did it.<|16.08|> <|16.68|> They were obedient to the dream.<|19.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.40|> The story also challenges my sometimes narrow perception of where God can be found.<|9.56|> <|10.58|> We Episcopalians have some very particular ways of experiencing God, especially on Sunday mornings.<|19.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.28|> In varying degrees of formality, we cross ourselves, we kneel, we might genuflect, we We have a pattern and a rhythm for God in our lives, both personally and particularly<|19.00|> <|19.00|> corporately.<|19.98|> <|19.98|> We have a pattern and a rhythm for God in our lives, both personally and particularly corporately.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Because the Episcopal expression of worship is so familiar and rich and comfortable for me,<|8.12|> <|8.94|> I sometimes forget that God speaks to people, and even to me if I'm really paying attention, in very different ways.<|20.96|> <|20.96|> Amen.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is no biblical or mythical reason to identify the Magi as Jews.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> They had no knowledge of Hebrew scripture.<|10.94|> <|11.54|> They were not looking for a Messiah.<|14.06|> <|15.60|> And yet they found him.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They traveled far because God called them on this journey, to this worship, to this witness of the newborn king, Jesus.<|11.16|> <|11.82|> God called them and directed them.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so, as God often does, God provided affirmation.<|5.56|> <|5.78|> The Magi announced the birth of the King of Jews,<|11.30|> <|11.96|> and the chief priests and scribes who did know Scripture<|15.94|> <|15.94|> immediately astronomers,<|9.10|> <|9.76|> men who worked in fields and men who studied the meanings of stars.<|15.20|> <|16.28|> These were the heralds and evangelists of the Messiah.<|20.12|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm not advising that we start looking for God in our horoscopes, but I do think we should acknowledge that God shows up in all sorts of places and <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> looking for God anywhere and everywhere,<|3.30|> <|3.94|> not just inside the walls of St. John's or any church,<|9.40|> <|10.00|> the Book of Common Prayer, or in the folks who sit around us,<|14.26|> <|15.04|> not just in the comfortable practices that we share every Sunday morning.<|19.98|> <|21.20|> She writes,<|22.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> -<|1.12|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> People encounter God under shady oak trees, on riverbanks, at the tops of mountains, and in long stretches of barren wilderness.<|11.60|> <|12.86|> God shows up in whirlwinds, starry skies, burning bushes, and perfect strangers.<|20.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.06|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When people want to know more about God, the Son of God tells them to pay attention to the lilies of the field and the birds of the air, to women needing bread and workers lining up for pay.<|16.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And she concludes that whoever wrote this stuff believed that people could learn as much about the ways of God from paying attention to the world as they could from paying attention to scripture.<|15.66|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Perhaps what we could learn from this epiphany story is that God can and does use the wildest things to bring people to God, even stars and astronomers.<|15.72|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.38|> Think, too, about the double blessing of this journey.<|4.14|> <|4.88|> On the way there to Bethlehem, they encountered people with whom they shared the mission of their journey.<|12.10|> <|13.44|> And on the way back, a whole new set of people because they had to travel by another road.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Their obedience and their flexibility allowed God to use them to protect the Messiah and to further spread word of him.<|10.16|> <|12.04|> It's interesting to think about what ways God might be able to use us.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If we followed that example, if we were willing to manage know that I have always wanted to be Nancy Drew.<|4.44|> <|6.02|> So it will come as no shock that these weeks of epiphany are my very favorite.<|14.40|> <|15.58|> If you too love a good mystery...<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You're going to love these coming weeks as the church explores the stories, the clues that reveal Jesus' divinity.<|11.56|> <|12.24|> It's the perfect time of year to practice being a detective of the divine, both inside church and outside our doors.<|23.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that was great<|3.64|><|endoftext|>
